title: The Nuclear Age and the Cold War
subject: History
caps_topic: The Nuclear Age and the Cold War

sources.title: Grade 9 - Term 2: The Nuclear Age and the Cold War
Hiroshima is targeted by a WWII atomic bomb
USA drops atomic bomb on Nagasaki
Winston Churchill announces that Britain has produced its own atomic bomb
Western countries, including South Africa, implement an airlift to supply provisions to the inhabitants of the eastern section of Berlin
Banishment under the Suppression of Communism Act of 1950
The United Nations and Apartheid Timeline 1946-1994

slides.title: The Nuclear Age and the Cold War
slides.lead: How a terrifying new weapon and a rivalry between two superpowers shaped the world - and reached all the way to South Africa.

media.alt: A globe split into two halves - one red, one blue - with a mushroom cloud rising behind it.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 320 180' role='img'><rect width='320' height='180' fill='#0e1b2a'/><path d='M120 40 q40 -30 80 0 q10 40 0 70 q-40 30 -80 0 q-10 -40 0 -70 Z' fill='#c9a94a' opacity='0.35'/><circle cx='160' cy='118' r='42' fill='#1f5b8a'/><path d='M160 76 a42 42 0 0 1 0 84 Z' fill='#8a1f1f'/><line x1='160' y1='72' x2='160' y2='164' stroke='#f4efe6' stroke-width='2'/><ellipse cx='160' cy='60' rx='26' ry='16' fill='#c9a94a' opacity='0.8'/><rect x='150' y='44' width='20' height='18' fill='#c9a94a' opacity='0.8'/></svg>

slides.notes: Open by asking learners what the word 'nuclear' makes them think of. Explain the two big ideas of this lesson: (1) the 'Nuclear Age' - the world after atomic weapons were first used in 1945, and (2) the 'Cold War' - a long rivalry between the United States and the Soviet Union that never became a direct 'hot' war between them but shaped nearly every part of the world, including Africa and South Africa.

slides.title: A new world after 1945
slides.lead: The Second World War ended in 1945 - and two 'superpowers' were left standing.

blocks.text: When the Second World War ended in 1945, most of Europe lay in ruins. Two countries were now far stronger than any other: the **United States of America (USA)** and the **Union of Soviet Socialist Republics (USSR, or Soviet Union)**. We call them **superpowers**.

items: They had been allies against Nazi Germany, but they did not trust each other.
They believed in opposite ways of running a country and an economy.
New organisations like the United Nations (1945) tried to keep the peace.

slides.notes: Stress the irony: the USA and USSR won the war together, then quickly became rivals. Explain 'superpower' - a country so powerful militarily and economically that its decisions affect the whole world. Mention the United Nations, which South Africa was a founding member of, as the new forum where these rivalries would often be argued out.

slides.title: Hiroshima and Nagasaki
slides.lead: For the first and only time in war, atomic bombs were dropped on two cities.

blocks.text: On **6 August 1945** the USA dropped an atomic bomb on the Japanese city of **Hiroshima**. Three days later, on **9 August 1945**, a second bomb destroyed **Nagasaki**. Japan surrendered soon after, ending the Second World War.

items: A single bomb could destroy an entire city in seconds.
By the end of 1945, an estimated 140,000 people in Hiroshima and 74,000 in Nagasaki had died from the blast, heat and radiation.
The world had entered the 'Nuclear Age' - and other countries now raced to build their own bombs.

media.alt: A mushroom cloud rising over a flattened city skyline.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 220 170' role='img'><rect width='220' height='170' fill='#0e1b2a'/><rect y='138' width='220' height='32' fill='#3a3a3a'/><g fill='#555'><rect x='16' y='124' width='16' height='14'/><rect x='40' y='118' width='14' height='20'/><rect x='176' y='122' width='16' height='16'/><rect x='196' y='128' width='12' height='10'/></g><ellipse cx='110' cy='46' rx='40' ry='26' fill='#e6b84a'/><ellipse cx='110' cy='40' rx='26' ry='16' fill='#f0d38a'/><rect x='98' y='60' width='24' height='58' fill='#e6b84a'/><ellipse cx='110' cy='122' rx='30' ry='12' fill='#c98a2a'/></svg>

slides.notes: Handle this slide with care and empathy - these were real people. The figures are widely cited estimates; exact numbers are uncertain because of the scale of destruction and later radiation deaths. Explain 'radiation' simply: an invisible poison released by the bomb that kept killing and making people ill for years. Make the link forward: the sheer power of these weapons is what made the Cold War so dangerous.

slides.title: Origins of the Cold War
slides.lead: A war of fear, threats and competition - not direct fighting between the superpowers.

blocks.text: The **Cold War** (about 1945 to 1991) was the tense rivalry between the USA and the USSR. It was called 'cold' because the two superpowers never fought each other directly - a direct war might have destroyed the world with nuclear weapons. Instead they competed through **spying, propaganda, alliances and the arms race**.

items: Europe was split into a Western bloc (led by the USA) and an Eastern bloc (led by the USSR).
In 1946 Winston Churchill said an 'Iron Curtain' had come down across Europe.
Each side tried to spread its own beliefs to other countries around the world.

media.alt: A map of Europe divided down the middle by a heavy iron curtain.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 160' role='img'><rect width='240' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect x='16' y='20' width='96' height='120' fill='#1f5b8a' opacity='0.7'/><rect x='128' y='20' width='96' height='120' fill='#8a1f1f' opacity='0.7'/><g fill='#3a3a3a'><rect x='115' y='14' width='10' height='132'/><rect x='113' y='14' width='14' height='6'/><rect x='113' y='24' width='14' height='6'/><rect x='113' y='34' width='14' height='6'/></g><text x='64' y='150' font-size='11' fill='#1f5b8a' text-anchor='middle' font-family='sans-serif'>West</text><text x='176' y='150' font-size='11' fill='#8a1f1f' text-anchor='middle' font-family='sans-serif'>East</text></svg>

slides.notes: Define 'bloc' - a group of countries on the same side. Explain the 'Iron Curtain' as an imaginary line, not a real wall, separating the free-market democracies of Western Europe from the Soviet-controlled communist states of Eastern Europe. Emphasise the central paradox: fear of total nuclear destruction is exactly what stopped the war from ever becoming 'hot' between the two superpowers.

slides.title: Capitalism versus Communism
slides.lead: At the heart of the Cold War were two very different ideas about how a country should be run.

items: Businesses and property are mostly privately owned.
Prices and wages are set by the market.
Usually linked to multi-party democracy and elections.
Led by the USA.
The state owns industry, land and property for 'the people'.
The government plans the economy.
Usually ruled by a single Communist Party.
Led by the USSR.

slides.notes: Keep this balanced and factual, not a value judgement - the point is that each side believed its system was better and feared the other would spread. Note that in reality neither side always lived up to its ideals. This clash of ideas (ideology) explains WHY the rivalry felt so total: it was a fight over how the whole world should be organised.

slides.title: Two armed camps
slides.lead: Both superpowers built military alliances to protect their bloc.

blocks.heading: NATO, 1949
blocks.text: The North Atlantic Treaty Organisation - the USA and its Western allies promised to defend one another.

blocks.heading: Warsaw Pact, 1955
blocks.text: The USSR and its Eastern European allies formed their own military alliance in reply.

blocks.heading: The whole world watches
blocks.text: Countries everywhere were pressured to pick a side - or to try to stay 'non-aligned'.

slides.notes: Explain that an alliance means an attack on one member counts as an attack on all. NATO came first (1949); the Warsaw Pact was the Soviet response (1955). Introduce 'non-aligned' - many newly independent countries, especially in Africa and Asia, tried not to join either side. This sets up the later slides on Africa.

slides.title: A divided city: Berlin
slides.lead: The German city of Berlin became one of the most dangerous places in the Cold War.

blocks.text: After the war, Germany and its capital Berlin were split between the superpowers. In **1948-1949** the USSR blockaded West Berlin, cutting off food and fuel. The West replied with the **Berlin Airlift**, flying in supplies day and night - and South African pilots took part in this airlift.

items: In 1961 the East built the **Berlin Wall** to stop people escaping to the West.
The Wall became the most famous symbol of a divided world.
When the Wall fell in 1989, it signalled that the Cold War was ending.

media.alt: A tall concrete wall topped with barbed wire dividing two sides.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 160' role='img'><rect width='240' height='160' fill='#cddbe6'/><rect y='118' width='240' height='42' fill='#9aa77f'/><rect x='100' y='30' width='40' height='100' fill='#c8c2b6'/><rect x='100' y='30' width='40' height='100' fill='none' stroke='#8a8477' stroke-width='2'/><g stroke='#3a3a3a' stroke-width='1.5' fill='none'><path d='M100 26 l40 0'/><path d='M104 20 l8 8 M112 20 l8 8 M120 20 l8 8 M128 20 l8 8'/></g><line x1='108' y1='42' x2='108' y2='130' stroke='#8a8477' stroke-width='1'/><line x1='120' y1='42' x2='120' y2='130' stroke='#8a8477' stroke-width='1'/><line x1='132' y1='42' x2='132' y2='130' stroke='#8a8477' stroke-width='1'/></svg>

slides.notes: Use the local connection - South African Air Force pilots flew in the Berlin Airlift - to show learners that even far-away South Africa was involved. Trace the arc of the Wall: built 1961 to trap people in the East, torn down 1989 as the Cold War collapsed. The Wall is a perfect concrete symbol of the 'Iron Curtain' idea from slide 4.

slides.title: The arms race and the space race
slides.lead: Both sides built more and more powerful weapons - enough to destroy the world many times over.

blocks.text: In the **arms race** each superpower tried to build more and deadlier weapons than the other, including the even more powerful **hydrogen bomb**. This created a frightening balance sometimes called 'mutually assured destruction' - if one side attacked, both would be destroyed.

items: By the 1960s both sides had thousands of nuclear missiles.
The rivalry spread to space: the USSR launched **Sputnik**, the first satellite, in 1957.
Schoolchildren in some countries practised 'duck and cover' drills in case of nuclear attack.

media.alt: Two missiles pointing at each other with a balance scale between them.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 160' role='img'><rect width='240' height='160' fill='#0e1b2a'/><g fill='#1f5b8a'><path d='M30 40 l0 60 l-10 14 l0 8 l30 0 l0 -8 l-10 -14 l0 -60 q-5 -14 0 -20 q5 6 0 20 Z'/></g><g fill='#8a1f1f'><path d='M210 40 l0 60 l-10 14 l0 8 l30 0 l0 -8 l-10 -14 l0 -60 q-5 -14 0 -20 q5 6 0 20 Z' transform='translate(-20,0)'/></g><line x1='120' y1='30' x2='120' y2='120' stroke='#c9a94a' stroke-width='3'/><line x1='90' y1='60' x2='150' y2='60' stroke='#c9a94a' stroke-width='3'/><circle cx='90' cy='60' r='8' fill='none' stroke='#c9a94a' stroke-width='3'/><circle cx='150' cy='60' r='8' fill='none' stroke='#c9a94a' stroke-width='3'/></svg>

slides.notes: Explain 'mutually assured destruction' (MAD) in simple terms: neither side dared to attack first because it would be destroyed too. This 'balance of terror' kept an uneasy peace. Link the arms race to the space race - Sputnik (1957) shocked the USA and pushed it to reach the Moon in 1969. The 'duck and cover' detail helps learners feel how ordinary people lived with nuclear fear.

slides.title: The Cuban Missile Crisis
slides.lead: For thirteen days in 1962 the world came closer to nuclear war than ever before.

blocks.text: In 1962 the USSR began placing nuclear missiles on the island of **Cuba**, just off the coast of the USA. US President **John F. Kennedy** demanded they be removed and blockaded the island. For thirteen tense days the world held its breath.

items: Soviet leader **Nikita Khrushchev** finally agreed to remove the missiles.
In return, the USA secretly agreed to remove missiles from Turkey and not to invade Cuba.
The crisis showed how easily the Cold War could have turned into a nuclear war.

media.alt: An island in the sea with a missile on it and a ship blockading the water around it.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 160' role='img'><rect width='240' height='160' fill='#1f5b8a'/><ellipse cx='120' cy='96' rx='60' ry='24' fill='#7a8b5a'/><path d='M118 96 l0 -34 l-6 8 l0 -12 l12 -14 l12 14 l0 12 l-6 -8 l0 34 Z' fill='#8a1f1f'/><g fill='#3a3a3a'><rect x='24' y='118' width='40' height='10'/><rect x='40' y='108' width='10' height='10'/><rect x='176' y='118' width='40' height='10'/><rect x='192' y='108' width='10' height='10'/></g></svg>

slides.notes: Set the scene: Cuba is a communist ally of the USSR right on the USA's doorstep, so Soviet missiles there terrified the USA. Name the two leaders - Kennedy (USA) and Khrushchev (USSR). Stress the deal that ended it was a compromise, not a victory for one side, and that some parts (removing US missiles from Turkey) were kept secret. This is the clearest example of how dangerous the arms race had become.

slides.title: The Cold War at a glance, 1945-1991

blocks.alt: A timeline of key Cold War events from 1945 to 1991.

events.label: Atomic bombs dropped on Hiroshima and Nagasaki; WWII ends
NATO formed; USSR tests its first atomic bomb
USSR launches Sputnik, the first satellite
The Soviet Union breaks up; the Cold War ends

slides.notes: Use this slide to consolidate before turning to Africa. Ask learners to spot the pattern: moments of great danger (1948, 1962) followed by uneasy calm, then a slow ending from 1989. Point out that the Cold War lasted about 46 years - longer than most of the learners' parents have been alive.

slides.title: A global contest in Africa
slides.lead: As African countries won independence, both superpowers competed for their friendship.

blocks.text: From the late 1950s many African colonies became independent nations. The USA and USSR both offered money, weapons and support to win these new countries to their side. Sometimes this competition helped fuel **proxy wars** - conflicts where the superpowers backed opposing local sides instead of fighting each other directly.

items: Many African leaders joined the **Non-Aligned Movement**, refusing to pick a side.
Civil wars in countries such as **Angola** and Mozambique drew in Cold War powers.
Cold War rivalry shaped aid, trade and conflict across the continent.

media.alt: An outline of the African continent with a blue arrow and a red arrow reaching towards it from opposite sides.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 160' role='img'><rect width='240' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><path d='M118 24 q22 4 24 22 q10 8 6 24 q8 14 -2 28 q2 18 -12 30 q-6 16 -14 6 q-10 2 -12 -12 q-14 -10 -12 -28 q-10 -14 0 -28 q0 -18 14 -26 q4 -18 18 -16 Z' fill='#9aa77f' stroke='#5f6b45' stroke-width='2'/><path d='M14 60 l40 12 l-8 6 l10 4 l-14 6' fill='none' stroke='#1f5b8a' stroke-width='4'/><path d='M226 100 l-40 -12 l8 -6 l-10 -4 l14 -6' fill='none' stroke='#8a1f1f' stroke-width='4'/></svg>

slides.notes: Define 'proxy war' clearly - the superpowers fought each other indirectly by arming and funding local groups. Explain 'Non-Aligned Movement' - a group of countries, many of them newly independent, that chose neither bloc. Angola is the key example to carry into the next slide about South Africa. Emphasise that decisions made in Washington and Moscow had real, sometimes deadly, effects on ordinary Africans.

slides.eyebrow: The Cold War and South Africa
slides.title: Apartheid and the Cold War
slides.lead: The apartheid government used Cold War fears to defend itself - and drew South Africa into a regional war.

blocks.text: South Africa's apartheid government presented itself to the West as a strong **anti-communist** ally. It used the fear of communism to justify crushing opposition - for example through the **Suppression of Communism Act of 1950**, which was written so broadly it could silence almost any critic of apartheid.

items: The government labelled many freedom fighters as 'communists' to discredit them.
South African forces fought in Angola, where they clashed with Cuban troops backing the other side.
As the Cold War ended around 1989-1990, some Western support for apartheid fell away, helping open the door to negotiations.

media.alt: A shield with the word anti-communism, casting a long shadow over a map of southern Africa.
media.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 240 160' role='img'><rect width='240' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><path d='M120 20 l46 16 l0 44 q0 40 -46 58 q-46 -18 -46 -58 l0 -44 Z' fill='#8a1f1f'/><path d='M120 20 l46 16 l0 44 q0 40 -46 58 Z' fill='#6f1717'/><line x1='96' y1='58' x2='144' y2='58' stroke='#f4efe6' stroke-width='4'/><line x1='120' y1='40' x2='120' y2='96' stroke='#f4efe6' stroke-width='4'/></svg>

slides.notes: This is the crucial 'so what for us?' slide. Two big ideas: (1) internally, apartheid leaders used the global fear of communism as an excuse to ban, jail and silence opponents - link back to the Suppression of Communism Act. (2) Externally, South Africa was pulled into a Cold War proxy conflict in Angola, even fighting Cuban troops. Flag for learners that calling opponents 'communist' was often a political tactic, not an accurate description. [SME] Confirm the level of detail expected on the Angola/Border War for Grade 9 and check the framing of shifting Western support around 1990.

slides.title: Review questions
slides.lead: Use what you have learned to answer these CAPS-style questions.

blocks.text: Answer in full sentences. The marks in brackets show how much detail is expected.

blocks.question: Explain why the rivalry between the USA and USSR was called a 'Cold' War.
Describe ONE flashpoint of the Cold War (Berlin OR Cuba) and explain why it was so dangerous.
Explain TWO ways the Cold War affected Africa or South Africa.

slides.notes: Use these as a written task or class discussion. The mark allocations follow CAPS source-based and paragraph-writing styles: 4-mark answers need a few clear points; the 6-mark answer needs a short paragraph with cause and effect. Encourage learners to use the key terms from the lesson: superpower, ideology, arms race, proxy war, anti-communism.

slides.title: Why the Nuclear Age and Cold War still matter
slides.lead: A rivalry that never became a direct war still changed the whole world - including our own country.

blocks.text: The atomic bombs of 1945 gave humans the power to destroy themselves, and the Cold War that followed divided the world for almost fifty years. It ended peacefully around **1989-1991** when the Berlin Wall fell and the Soviet Union broke apart.

items: Nuclear weapons still exist, so the choices of the Nuclear Age remain with us today.
The Cold War shaped independence, wars and politics across Africa.
In South Africa, its ending helped create space for the move towards democracy in the 1990s.

slides.notes: Close by returning to the two threads from slide 1: the Nuclear Age and the Cold War. Underline the hopeful point that the Cold War ended without the feared nuclear catastrophe, and mostly peacefully. Connect to learners' own lives: the democratic South Africa they know grew partly out of a world that was changing as the Cold War ended. This links forward to the Grade 9 Term 3 unit on turning points in South African history.
